methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $250M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Biotech & Health. Range: $810M - $4.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 6.1x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: New Limit
private.rationale: Same sector (Biotech & Health), same stage (Series C), post-money $810M

data.narrative:
eGenesis, a Series C biotech company focused on cellular and gene therapy applications, is evaluated against a comparable universe of 8 private and 3 public firms in the Biotech & Health sector.

Two valuation methods triangulate a reasonable range. Stage-progression analysis, applying a 1.7x step-up multiplier to the company's $250M+ total raised, implies $425M. Peer-median anchoring across four same-stage private comps—Ultima Genomics ($4.0B), Insitro ($2.4B), Colossal Biosciences ($1.5B), and New Limit ($810M)—yields a median valuation of $1.9B, with a range of $810M to $4.0B. Public-comp multiples (median P/S of 6.1x across RXRX, TEM, and ONT.L) cannot be applied without disclosed revenue figures. The stage-progression method appears conservative relative to peer comparables, likely reflecting the timing of capital deployment versus later valuations achieved by peers in similar discovery and development phases.

Triangulating both applicable methods produces a valuation range of $361M (low), $1.2B (median), to $2.2B (high). The median reflects equal weighting of the two approaches. Key uncertainties include: the magnitude of technical risk in xenotransplantation and immune tolerance; competitive positioning relative to Ultima and Insitro; and regulatory pathway clarity for clinical advancement. The wide spread between stage-progression and peer-median suggests material optionality in near-term capital rounds and clinical or partnership announcements.
